text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
SURVEILLANCE / RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION: 59-year-old female with known history of BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma (Stage IV, M1c) with prior hepatic, bilateral axillary lymph node, right inguinal lymph node, and subcutaneous metastatic involvement. Currently on Opdivo maintenance monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (approximately 6 months of single-agent maintenance following 2 cycles of ipi/nivo induction; approximately 9 months total from first immunotherapy dose). Prior treatment with dabra/Mekinist (first-line, discontinued at 8 months due to progression). Surveillance imaging to assess treatment response.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast, with acquisition in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and standard phase for the chest. Coronal and sagittal reformats were generated and reviewed. Comparison is made to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 3 months ago, at which time there was a documented partial response with left axillary lymph node measuring 1.3 cm, a dominant hepatic lesion in segment VI measuring 0.9 cm, a segment II hepatic lesion measuring 0.6 cm, and a right inguinal lymph node measuring 0.5 cm.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, areas of consolidation, ground-glass opacification, or pleural-based lesions are identified. The airways are patent to the level of the subsegmental bronchi. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleura and Pericardium: No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion.

Mediastinum: The mediastinal contour is within normal limits. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thymus appears appropriate for age. The thoracic aorta and great vessels are unremarkable in caliber and contour.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesions. No axillary lymphadenopathy on the right. On the left, there is a single left axillary lymph node measuring approximately 0.8 cm in short axis (previously 1.3 cm on the prior study approximately 3 months ago, and previously 3.4 cm at the time of documented disease progression on dabra/tram). This lymph node has a preserved fatty hilum and normal morphology, with no internal necrosis, irregular margins, or concerning cortical thickening. The appearance is markedly improved from prior studies and this node now falls within the range of a normal-to-borderline reactive lymph node. No other enlarged or morphologically suspicious left axillary lymph nodes are identified.

Skeleton (chest): No suspicious osseous lesions in the imaged thoracic skeleton. No lytic or sclerotic lesions. Vertebral body heights are preserved.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal parenchymal lesions. In particular, careful evaluation of segment VI — the site of the previously identified dominant hepatic metastasis measuring up to 3.2 cm at initial staging — reveals only a tiny focus of subtle hypoenhancement/low attenuation measuring approximately 0.4 cm, markedly diminished compared to the 0.9 cm lesion identified at the prior scan three months ago and representing near-complete resolution of what was previously the index hepatic target lesion. This residual focus is at the threshold of reliable measurement and may represent post-treatment fibrosis, a residual treated lesion, or simply volume averaging artifact; it is not confidently characterizable as active metastatic disease given its size and imaging characteristics.

The previously identified segment II hepatic lesion (measuring 0.6 cm on the prior study) is no longer identified on the current examination. No discrete hypodense or hypervascular nodule is seen in this location. The segment IVa lesion, which had already resolved on the prior scan, likewise remains absent.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent. No perihepatic ascites.

Biliary: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. The common bile duct is normal in caliber.

Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and enhancement throughout the head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic lymphadenopathy.

Spleen: Normal in size at approximately 9 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No splenic lesions. No splenic hilar lymphadenopathy.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits in size and configuration. No adrenal nodules or masses to suggest metastatic involvement.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and are normal in size. No renal masses or hydronephrosis. Bilateral small cortical cysts are noted, unchanged from prior studies, consistent with benign simple cysts. No perinephric stranding.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The visualized portions of the bowel demonstrate no wall thickening, pneumatosis, or obstruction. The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel loops appear normal. No free intraperitoneal air. Given the patient's prior history of immune-related colitis, the colonic wall thickness is specifically evaluated and appears normal throughout, without mucosal hyperenhancement, haustral thickening, or pericolonic fat stranding — features reassuring against active colitis.

Mesentery and Peritoneum: No peritoneal nodularity, implants, or ascites to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. Mesenteric fat is clear.

Abdominal Aorta and Vessels: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilation. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS:

Lymphatics: The previously documented right inguinal lymph node (0.5 cm on prior scan, down from 1.2 cm at progression) now measures approximately 0.3 cm or is not confidently identified as a discrete lymph node on the current examination. No inguinal, iliac, or obturator lymphadenopathy is identified bilaterally. No pelvic sidewall lymphadenopathy.

Pelvic Organs: The uterus is normal in size and configuration for age. No adnexal lesions. The urinary bladder is adequately distended and shows no wall irregularity or intraluminal filling defect. No pelvic free fluid.

Musculoskeletal (abdomen/pelvis): No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions in the imaged lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvic bones. No pathological fractures.

Skin and Subcutaneous Tissues: No subcutaneous nodules are identified along the left flank or elsewhere in the imaged soft tissues. The previously identified left flank subcutaneous nodule (measuring up to 1.8 cm at initial staging and 0.5 cm on the prior scan) is not seen on the current examination. The overlying skin and subcutaneous fat are unremarkable.

LABORATORY CORRELATION (provided for imaging context): LDH reported as 138 U/L, within the laboratory reference range.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Near-complete imaging response to immunotherapy across all previously documented sites of metastatic disease. Compared to the prior CT performed approximately three months ago, and compared to the baseline of disease progression prior to immunotherapy, there has been continued and dramatic reduction in tumor burden.

2. Left axillary lymphadenopathy: The previously enlarged and bulky left axillary lymph node has diminished further to 0.8 cm (short axis) with restoration of a normal fatty hilum, representing an approximately 76% reduction from the 3.4 cm size documented at immunotherapy initiation. The node is now morphologically indeterminate — it may represent a small residual focus of treated disease, a reactive lymph node, or post-treatment fibrotic change. No other left axillary adenopathy.

3. Hepatic metastases: The dominant segment VI hepatic lesion has decreased to a tiny 0.4 cm focus of uncertain character, down from 0.9 cm on the prior scan and from 3.2 cm at initial staging. The segment II hepatic lesion has resolved and is no longer identifiable. The segment IVa lesion remains absent, as on the prior examination. No new hepatic lesions.

4. Subcutaneous disease: The previously documented left flank subcutaneous nodule is no longer identified.

5. Right inguinal lymph node: No longer identifiable as a discrete structure; essentially resolved.

6. No new sites of metastatic disease. No pulmonary nodules. No intrathoracic adenopathy. No osseous lesions. No peritoneal disease.

7. LDH within normal limits (138 U/L), consistent with the imaging findings of marked treatment response.

Overall, the pattern of continued regression across all nodal, visceral, and subcutaneous sites is highly consistent with an ongoing and deepening response to Opdivo maintenance immunotherapy. The minimal residual findings in the left axilla and liver are at or near the limits of CT resolution and cannot reliably be distinguished from fibrotic or reactive post-treatment change versus submillimeter residual viable disease.
